p: A lightweight Windows desktop text-to-speech application for fast, reliable voice communication in Discord, VRChat, and other voice-enabled applications.
Main application showing the system tray icon, overlay input window, and settings window demonstrating the core workflow.

TTS Communication Tool is a communication prosthetic for mute users who need instant, reliable speech in live voice conversations. It lives unobtrusively in the system tray and provides a hotkey-driven overlay for typing messages that are spoken aloud through both your headphones and a virtual audio cable (used as microphone input by Discord, VRChat, etc.).
Getting Started — Installation, requirements, and first-run setup
User Guide — Complete feature documentation for end users
Install VB-Cable (or any virtual audio cable)
Launch the app — it appears in the system tray
On first run, the Settings window opens automatically
Select your Monitor Output (headphones/speakers) and Secondary Output (CABLE Input)
Press Ctrl+Shift+Space to open the overlay, type a message, press Enter
Getting Started — Installation, requirements, and first-run setup
User Guide — Complete feature documentation for end users
This application is Windows-only. It uses WPF, NAudio (WASAPI), and Win32 P/Invoke for global hotkeys — none of which work on Linux or macOS.
Place it anywhere on your system (no installer required)
The app requires a virtual audio cable to route TTS audio into voice applications.
Verify "CABLE Input" and "CABLE Output" appear in your Windows sound devices
Other virtual audio cables work too, including:
In Discord, VRChat, or any voice app:
Set Microphone Input to CABLE Output (VB-Audio Virtual Cable)
Keep Speaker Output set to your normal headphones/speakers
When you launch the app for the first time:
The app appears in the system tray (near the clock)
Select your Secondary Output device (CABLE Input)
First-run settings window showing device selection dropdowns and test buttons.
To update to a new version:
Close the running app (right-click tray icon → Exit)
Replace the old executable with the new one
Your settings and phrases are stored in %AppData%\TtsCommunicationTool\ and are preserved across updates.
Get from a fresh install to speaking your first message in under two minutes.
Run TtsCommunicationTool.App.exe. The app starts in the system tray.
System tray area showing the TTS Communication Tool icon (a speech bubble icon) highlighted.
On first launch, the Settings window opens automatically:
Monitor Output — Select your headphones or speakers
Secondary Output — Select CABLE Input (VB-Audio Virtual Cable)
Click Test Monitor to hear a test tone on your headphones
Click Test Secondary to route a test tone through the virtual cable
If the Settings window doesn't open, right-click the tray icon and select Settings.

l: Step 3: Set Up Your Voice App
p: In Discord, VRChat, or your voice app:
Set Microphone to CABLE Output (VB-Audio Virtual Cable)
Keep Speaker as your normal output device
Press Ctrl+Shift+Space — a dark overlay window appears
Press Enter — the message is spoken aloud
The overlay window with typed text and the Send button visible.
Press Ctrl+Shift+Backspace to immediately stop any speech that's playing.
The app stores all settings in a JSON configuration file. Most settings are managed through the Settings window, but understanding the file structure helps with troubleshooting and backup.
On most systems this resolves to:

c:

p: If the config file becomes corrupt (e.g., from a disk error or manual editing mistake):
The app detects the corruption on next launch
A backup is saved as config.corrupt.timestamp.json
The config is reset to defaults
A notification is shown: "Configuration file was corrupt and has been reset to defaults. A backup was saved."
Do not edit the config file while the app is running. The app overwrites the file on save, which could cause data loss.
The TTS Communication Tool is designed around a simple, fast workflow. This guide covers every user-facing feature.
Features — Complete list of all features
TTS Workflow — Detailed walkthrough of the speech pipeline
Voices — Kokoro offline voices and ElevenLabs integration
Audio Output — Dual-output routing, volume, and silence trimming
Shortcuts — All keyboard shortcuts and hotkeys
Phrase System — Saved phrases, categories, favorites, and import/export
Text Replacements — Automatic text substitution rules
Themes — Visual customization and theme management
Press a configurable global hotkey (default: Ctrl+Shift+Space) from anywhere to open a dark, always-on-top input overlay. The overlay auto-focuses the text input so you can start typing immediately.
Press Enter to send. The Kokoro offline TTS engine generates speech audio and plays it to both your monitoring output and the virtual cable simultaneously.
Audio is played simultaneously to two outputs:
Monitor Output — Your headphones or speakers (you hear the speech)
Secondary Output — A virtual audio cable (voice apps hear the speech)
Press Ctrl+Shift+Backspace to immediately halt any speech that's playing on both outputs.
The app lives in the system tray. Right-click the tray icon for:
Open Phrase Manager — Manage saved phrases
Reset Theme to Default — Revert to the Default Dark theme
Kokoro runs entirely on-device. No internet connection or API subscriptions are required for the default voice.
Connect your ElevenLabs account for premium AI voices. The API key is encrypted with DPAPI and stored securely.
Save frequently-used messages as phrases. Trigger them instantly from the Phrase Manager or assign hotkeys to individual phrases.
Organize phrases into categories, mark favorites, and pin important phrases to the top of the list.
A dedicated window for creating and editing phrases with per-phrase voice overrides (engine, voice, pitch) and TTS preview.
Define automatic text substitution rules that run before TTS synthesis. Supports case-sensitive matching, whole-word matching, and priority ordering.
Customize the entire visual appearance with built-in themes (Default Dark, Default Light, Midnight Purple, Nord) or create your own. Full colour editor with WCAG 2.1 AA contrast checking.
Set independent volume levels for the monitor output and secondary output (0–100%).
Adjust the pitch of all TTS output (0.5x–2.0x). Changes apply live without saving.
Automatically remove trailing silence from generated audio to reduce delays between messages.
Optionally log all spoken text to a timestamped transcript file at %AppData%\TtsCommunicationTool\transcript.txt.
Structured JSONL session logs for debugging. Configurable verbosity levels (INFO, DEBUG, TRACE) with optional request ID correlation.
The last 20 sent messages are tracked in memory (not persisted). Duplicate consecutive entries are collapsed.
Resend the last spoken message via a configurable hotkey or the overlay's resend button.
The overlay remembers its last position on screen across sessions.
Shows a live countdown of remaining playback time in the overlay while speech is playing.
Optionally preserve unsent overlay text when the overlay closes without sending.
Optionally enforce a maximum character limit (default: 500) on overlay input.
The settings UI warns you if you try to assign the same hotkey to multiple actions.
Import and export phrases as JSON files for backup or sharing.
Import and export themes as .ttstheme JSON files.
An optional splash screen shown on startup (can be disabled in settings).
The Settings window opens automatically on first launch, guiding you through device selection and testing.
This page explains the complete text-to-speech pipeline from keypress to audio output.
The user types text in the overlay window. The overlay validates:
Text is not empty or whitespace-only
Text does not exceed the character limit (if enabled)
If text replacements are enabled, the input text passes through the TextReplacementService. Rules are applied in sort order. Earlier rules take priority over overlapping later rules.
The system determines which voice to use:
For standard overlay input: uses the globally configured voice and pitch
For phrases: uses per-phrase overrides if set, otherwise falls back to global settings
The TtsRouter selects the appropriate engine:
Kokoro (default): Offline synthesis via KokoroSharp. The model (~320MB) auto-downloads on first use.
ElevenLabs (optional): Cloud API synthesis. Requires an API key and internet connection.
The DualOutputAudioRouter receives the PCM audio data and:
Creates a WaveFormat from the audio metadata (sample rate, bit depth, channels)
Fans the same audio buffer to two NAudio WasapiOut streams
If the stop hotkey is pressed during playback:
StopAll() cancels the playback cancellation token
Both NAudio streams are stopped and disposed
The PlaybackFinished event is suppressed (to distinguish forced stop from natural completion)
Text replacements are applied in a single pass:
All enabled rules are collected and sorted by SortOrder
Each rule finds matches using regex (case-insensitive by default, whole-word optional)
Earlier rules claim their match spans first
Later rules skip any text that overlaps with already-claimed spans
The output is built by walking through substitutions in position order
Global pitch (0.5–2.0) is applied to all standard overlay TTS
Phrases always use pitch 1.0 (neutral) unless a per-phrase override is set
Pitch is passed as a TtsRequest.Pitch value and handled by the TTS engine
After TTS synthesis, the raw PCM data is scanned backwards from the end
Frames where all samples are below the silence threshold (~0.5% amplitude) are identified
A configurable fraction of the trailing silence is retained (0–100%)
The trimmed audio is passed to the audio router for playback
The app supports two TTS engines: Kokoro (offline, default) and ElevenLabs (cloud, optional).
Kokoro is the default TTS engine. It runs entirely offline — no internet connection required.
Kokoro provides a variety of voices loaded from bundled voice model files. The default voice is AF Heart (af_heart).
Additional voices loaded from Kokoro voice files

The exact list of available Kokoro voices depends on the voice files present in the KokoroSharp installation. The app calls KokoroVoiceManager.LoadVoicesFromPath() during initialization to discover available voices.
If the configured voice ID is not found, the app falls back to af_heart. If even the default voice is missing, synthesis fails with a clear error message.
ElevenLabs provides high-quality cloud-based AI voices. This is an optional integration that requires:
An API key (stored encrypted with DPAPI)
Click Enter API Key and paste your key
The app fetches your available voices and subscription info
Select a voice from the list
Voice settings tab showing ElevenLabs engine selected with API key entry field and voice dropdown.
The API key is encrypted using Windows DPAPI (DataProtectionScope.CurrentUser)
Only the last 4 characters are stored in plain text (for display purposes)
The encrypted key is stored in config.json as elevenLabs.encryptedApiKey
Legacy plain-text keys are migrated to encrypted storage on first load
After entering a valid API key, the app displays:
Cumulative characters used across all sessions
ElevenLabs voices marked as IsCustomVoice (user-cloned or AI-generated) are highlighted in red in the UI. These require a Creator or higher ElevenLabs subscription.
The default ElevenLabs model is eleven_multilingual_v2. This can be changed in the Voice settings tab.
A global pitch multiplier affects all standard TTS output:
Changes apply live — no save required
Phrases always use pitch 1.0 unless a per-phrase override is set
Each phrase can override the global voice settings:
Engine override: Use Kokoro or ElevenLabs for a specific phrase
Voice override: Use a specific voice for a specific phrase
Pitch override: Set a per-phrase pitch multiplier
These are configured in the Phrase Editor window.
The app plays audio simultaneously to two independent output devices:
The primary output device — typically your headphones or speakers. This is where you hear the speech.
The virtual audio cable input. Voice applications (Discord, VRChat) use this as their microphone input.
Independent volume sliders for each output:
Volumes are applied as float multipliers (0.0–1.0) to the NAudio WasapiOut stream.
Monitor Output dropdown — lists all active WASAPI render devices
Secondary Output dropdown — lists all active WASAPI render devices
Refresh button — re-enumerates devices (useful after plugging/unplugging)

Device IDs are stored as WASAPI device identifiers. If a device is disconnected or drivers change, the saved ID may become invalid. The app will show a warning and you'll need to re-select the device.
Three test buttons help verify your audio setup:
Plays a short test tone to the monitor output only
Plays a short test tone to the secondary output only
Plays a short test tone to both outputs simultaneously
When enabled, the app automatically removes trailing silence from generated TTS audio:
Trim Trailing Silence: Enable/disable the feature
Silence Retention: What percentage of trailing silence to keep (5–100%)
After TTS synthesis, the raw 16-bit PCM data is scanned backwards from the end
Frames where all samples are below the silence threshold (~0.5% of max amplitude) are identified
The specified fraction of trailing silence is retained
The trimmed audio is passed to the audio router
Enabling silence trimming can significantly reduce the gap between pressing Enter and hearing speech, especially with voices that produce long trailing silences.
IsPlaying — whether audio is currently playing
This state is shared between the overlay and phrase playback, ensuring the playback timer and status display are consistent.
The Settings window provides access to all configuration options. Open it by:
Settings window showing the tabbed interface with General, Hotkeys, Audio, Voice, Phrases, Appearance, and Theme tabs.
Launch the app automatically when you log in
Limit overlay input to max characters
Log all spoken text to transcript.txt
Maximum characters when limit is enabled
Preserve unsent text when overlay closes
Show remaining time in overlay during playback
Show the splash screen on startup
Attach correlation IDs to TTS pipeline events
Enabling "Log raw text" will write the actual spoken text to log files. This can capture sensitive or private information. Only enable for debugging specific issues, and disable it afterward.
At least one modifier (Ctrl, Alt, Shift) is required
Configure global hotkeys for various actions:
Duplicate hotkeys are detected and rejected
Reserved keys (LeftCtrl, RightAlt, etc.) cannot be used alone
Stop current audio and send overlay text immediately
Win key is not supported as a modifier
Remove silence from end of audio
See Audio Output for full details.
Opacity changes are previewed live on the open overlay window.
See Phrase System for full details.
See Text Replacements for full details.
The Save button persists all changes to config.json
Hotkeys are re-registered after save (no restart needed)
A save toast notification appears (debounced to prevent spam)
The Cancel button reverts all unsaved changes
The Reset Defaults button resets all settings to factory defaults
These hotkeys work from anywhere in Windows, even when other applications are focused.
Stops current audio, then sends overlay text
These hotkeys work while the overlay window is open.
The phrase system lets you save frequently-used messages and trigger them instantly.
Phrase Manager showing the list of saved phrases with search, category filter, and favorites toggle.
Optional global hotkey to trigger this phrase
Pin to top of the list
Update — Save changes to an existing phrase
Play — Trigger TTS for the selected phrase immediately
Import — Import phrases from a JSON file
Export — Export phrases to a JSON file
Search box — Filter phrases by text or name
Category filter — Show only phrases in a specific category
Favorites toggle — Show only favorite phrases
The Phrase Editor is a dedicated window for detailed phrase editing.
Phrase Editor window showing text fields, voice override options, and preview controls.
Each phrase can override the global voice configuration:
Force Kokoro or ElevenLabs for this phrase
Use a specific voice for this phrase
Play Preview — Synthesize and play the phrase with current settings
Regen Cache — Regenerate the cached audio file

The preview cache stores the last synthesized audio. Changing any setting (text, engine, voice, pitch) invalidates the cache, ensuring you never hear stale audio. This also prevents unnecessary ElevenLabs API calls.
Phrases are pre-cached as WAV files on disk for instant playback:
Generated: Automatically when a phrase is added or updated
Used: On phrase playback — if cache exists, no TTS synthesis is needed
Phrases are exported as a JSON array:

c:
[
  {
    "id": "guid-here",
    "name": "Greeting",
    "text": "Hello everyone!",
    "category": "Social",
    "isFavorite": true,
    "isPinned": false,
    "sortOrder": 1,
    "hotkey": null,
    "overrideEngine": null,
    "useVoiceOverride": false,
    "overrideVoiceId": null,
    "overrideVoiceName": null,
    "overridePitch": null,
    "createdUtc": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
    "updatedUtc": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z"
  }
]

p: Phrases are added to the existing list (not replaced)
If a phrase name conflicts with an existing phrase, the imported name is adjusted
Hotkeys from imported phrases are cleared to prevent conflicts
A summary is shown: how many added, names changed, hotkeys cleared
The last 20 sent messages are tracked in memory (not persisted to disk):
Accessible via the overlay's resend button or Resend hotkey
Text replacements automatically transform your input text before it's sent to the TTS engine. This is useful for expanding abbreviations, correcting common typos, or substituting words.
You type text in the overlay
Before TTS synthesis, the TextReplacementService applies all enabled rules
The transformed text is sent to the TTS engine
The original text in the overlay is unchanged
Replacements tab showing a list of text replacement rules with trigger/replacement fields and enable toggles.
Add — Create a new replacement rule
Import — Import rules from a JSON file
Export — Export rules to a JSON file
Rules are processed in a single pass:
All enabled rules are sorted by their internal sort order
Each rule finds all matches in the input text
Earlier rules claim their match positions first
Later rules skip any text that overlaps with already-claimed positions
The output is built by walking through all substitutions in position order
Add important rules with lower sort order numbers so they take priority over overlapping matches from later rules.
"btw, I'm here" → "by the way, I'm here"

The entire text replacement system can be enabled or disabled with the Enable Replacements toggle at the top of the Replacements tab. When disabled, no rules are applied regardless of individual rule states.
The theme system allows complete visual customization of the application.
Theme editor showing the colour swatches, preview pane, and preset dropdown.
Window, Panel, Deep Panel, History, Surface 0, Border, Surface 2
Info, Error, Error Hover, Warning, Success
Overlay Background, Overlay Border, Overlay Opacity, Overlay Corner Radius
Each colour swatch can be edited by:
Clicking the swatch — Opens the Windows native colour picker dialog
Clicking the eyedropper button — Opens a screen colour picker to sample any pixel on screen
Typing a hex value — Directly enter #RRGGBB values
The theme editor includes a built-in contrast checker that evaluates colour pairs against WCAG 2.1 AA standards:
The contrast ratio is calculated using the relative luminance formula from the WCAG 2.1 specification.
Select any theme from the dropdown to load it into the editor. Changes are previewed live.
Save As — Save the current theme as a new user theme
Duplicate — Create a copy of the current theme and switch to it immediately
User-created themes can be deleted (with confirmation). Built-in themes cannot be deleted.
Export — Save a theme as a .ttstheme JSON file
Import — Load a theme from a .ttstheme file (validates required fields)
Right-click the tray icon → Reset Theme to Default to immediately revert to the Default Dark theme.
User themes are stored as individual .ttstheme JSON files:
Built-in themes are defined in code (ThemeDefaults class) and always appear before user themes in the preset list.
Themes are applied via IThemeService.Apply() which:
Maps each ThemeSettings property to a WPF DynamicResource key
Creates SolidColorBrush objects from hex colour strings
All XAML elements using {DynamicResource ...} update automatically
The app supports importing and exporting data for portability and backup.
Import and Export buttons appear in the bottom action bar of the Settings window when the active tab supports them:
Exports all phrases as a JSON array. Each phrase includes all fields (name, text, category, favorites, voice overrides, etc.).
Phrases are added to the existing list
Imported hotkeys are cleared to prevent conflicts
A summary dialog shows: count added, names changed, hotkeys cleared
Saves the current theme as a .ttstheme JSON file. All colour, typography, shape, and overlay settings are included.
Validates all required fields on load
Returns a user-editable theme (not applied automatically)
Must be saved via the theme editor to persist
Exports all replacement rules as a JSON array.
Replaces the current rule list with the imported rules.
Symptoms: App starts but shows error: "TTS engine failed to initialize"
Kokoro model file (kokoro.onnx) is missing or corrupt
Insufficient disk space for model download (~320MB)
Ensure kokoro.onnx exists in the app directory
Re-download the app (the model is bundled)
Symptoms: Notification on startup: "Configuration file was corrupt and has been reset to defaults"
Manual editing of config.json while the app was running
Disk error or power loss during config save
A backup was saved as config.corrupt.timestamp.json
Restore settings from the backup if needed
Re-configure the app through the Settings window
Symptoms: Pressing the overlay hotkey does nothing
Another application has registered the same hotkey
The hotkey was not configured (empty binding)
Try a different hotkey combination in Settings → Hotkeys
Check for conflicting apps (e.g., Discord, OBS, game overlays)
Symptoms: Speech plays but you can't hear it
Monitor output device not selected or disconnected
Verify the Monitor Output dropdown has a device selected
Symptoms: You can hear speech through your headphones, but others can't hear you
Voice app microphone set to wrong device
Verify VB-Cable is installed (check Windows Sound settings)
Select CABLE Input (VB-Audio Virtual Cable) as Secondary Output
In Discord/VRChat, set Microphone to CABLE Output (VB-Audio Virtual Cable)
Symptoms: Error when trying to use ElevenLabs voice
Verify the key is active in your ElevenLabs account
Rate limited — too many requests in a short time
Wait a few seconds and try again
Check your ElevenLabs subscription character usage in Settings → Voice
Symptoms: Error with voice ID in the message
The selected voice no longer exists (deleted from ElevenLabs)
Click to refresh the voice list
Symptoms: Clicking a phrase does nothing
Check the app is fully started (tray icon visible)
Open the Phrase Editor and click Play Preview
Check logs for cache generation errors
Symptoms: Hotkey pressed but no overlay
App minimized to tray but not responding
Check if the overlay is already open (it may be behind another window)
Check logs for hotkey registration errors
Symptoms: Double-clicking the EXE does nothing
Another instance is already running (single-instance enforcement)
Check if the app is already running (check system tray)
Check Windows Event Viewer for crash details
Symptoms: Message box: "TTS Swirlotl is already running."
Cause: The app uses a named mutex (Global\TtsCommunicationTool_SingleInstance) to enforce single-instance. Only one instance can run at a time.
Fix: Look for the running instance in the system tray.
Check Windows volume mixer — ensure the app isn't muted
Verify audio devices are selected in Settings → Audio
Click Test Monitor and Test Secondary to isolate the issue

l: Audio Only Plays on One Output
p: Verify both device IDs are saved (check config.json)
Click Test Both to verify dual-output routing
Reduce the sample rate or bit depth (not configurable in current version)
Check for driver issues with your audio device
Enable Trim Trailing Silence in Settings → Audio
Set Silence Retention to a lower value (e.g., 5%)
Use Kokoro (offline) instead of ElevenLabs (cloud API has network latency)
If still missing, check Windows Sound settings → Playback devices
The playback timer estimates remaining time based on audio duration. If the timer seems off:
It's calculated from PlaybackDurationSeconds minus elapsed time
The timer stops when PlaybackFinished fires
If audio is stopped early (stop hotkey), the timer disappears
If pressing the stop hotkey doesn't stop audio:
Check the stop hotkey is configured in Settings → Hotkeys
Check for hotkey conflicts with other apps
Symptoms: "Failed to load runtime" or app won't start
Symptoms: No secondary output device available in settings
Solution: Download and install from VB-Audio
Solution: The model (~320MB) auto-downloads on first run. Ensure:
This application is Windows-only and will not run on Linux or macOS. It depends on:
WPF (Windows Presentation Foundation) for the UI
NAudio WASAPI for audio device enumeration and playback
Windows DPAPI for API key encryption
The app works on both Windows 10 and Windows 11 (x64). No known differences in behavior.

Support for overlay display over exclusive fullscreen applications (games) is best-effort only. The overlay uses Topmost=True which works with borderless fullscreen but may not appear over true exclusive fullscreen.
The user must install a virtual audio cable separately. The app does not bundle or install one. Common options:
Block the Kokoro model download (large file, network activity)
Flag the app as suspicious (uses global hotkeys, system tray)
Add an exception for the app directory if needed.
The app targets net10.0-windows. The .NET 10 runtime must be installed. Release builds may bundle the runtime as a self-contained deployment.

The app uses a named mutex (Global\TtsCommunicationTool_SingleInstance) to enforce single-instance. Only one instance can run at a time. Attempting to launch a second instance shows a message box and exits.
